Jon: 	Now, we’re going to go into the 3X VSL Toolbox.
Where are you going to use those comparisons? We will cover it in the
next session. You just want to write your too light and too heavy, and
don’t get too caught up in details about this. Just get some ideas
flowing.
Remember, this is kind of the sauce and the spice of your VSL.
Okay, let’s get into the toolbox. These are the tools that are absolutely
necessary for you to have a winning video sales letter. Every one of
these tools are going to be covered over the next few sessions, and
we’re going to show you exactly how and when to implement them.
We want to prepare you ahead of time for what these are.
Don’t worry about having to do anything, right now. We just want you
to know what the tools are.
The first tool is called the paraphrase strategy. Every 5th or 7th slide,
anywhere between 5 and 7 slides, what you’re going to do is you’re
going to break your pattern from using every word that you’re saying
on the screen to using only a paraphrased version of that material on
the slide. We’ll give you an example, and then I’ll tell you why.
I can rattle on and say, “Using the Caloric Staggering Principle, what’s
going to happen is your metabolism is going to do this, that, and the
other, and blah, blah, blah.” All you see on the screen here is “This
helps you lose weight.”
I just paraphrased what I was saying. Or, “It really speeds things up.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 12 --

2
Here’s the reason you want to do this. The reason you want to do this
is if you always have every word on the slide, people who read faster,
especially, are going to get to the end of the slide before you do, and
they’re going to get bored.
But if you break this up every 5 slides or so, with a paraphrase, they
don’t know when your sentence is going to end, and this keeps them
hypnotically glued to the screen.
I’ve actually done VSL’s where the whole thing was paraphrased. It
actually did quite well. That is a technique that I do teach. It’s not as
easy to do as the technique we’re talking about here, where every
word is on the screen, but here’s the great split down the middle
benefit for both. Every 5th slide, you do the paraphrase; it creates a
pattern break, and they don’t know where you’re going to finish. They
don’t know what’s coming next, so it makes everything stay very
interesting and topical. Does that make sense?
Okay, let’s go on to the next tip. The next tool is the headline benefit
formula. This is a formula that you can use to write any headline you
ever have to write. If you’re writing a text-based sales page, if you’re
writing a video sales letter, this is going to be used in the headline
above your video sales letter, because there is going to be one. This
is going to be used as a way to phrase your benefits.
There are so many applications for this very, very simple formula.
Here it is. Power word plus outrageous result, minus public enemy #1.
You can even do public enemy #1, #2, #3.
Everyone’s public enemy is going to be different. This is your public’s
enemy, your customer’s worst enemies.
So, power word with outrageous results, outrageous benefits added,
minus the public enemy. This is going to look very familiar. This is
also, like I said, perfect for your VSL, perfect for your big promise,
which we’ll cover in a minute, the benefits, and more.
Here’s an example: “3 ways to lose weight quickly and easily, without
giving up your favorite foods or spending hours doing cardio.” It’s a
very simple headline formula.
What do I have here? I have power words, “quickly and easily.” I told
what it is, “3 ways to lose weight, quickly and easily.” “Quickly and
easily” are the power words. “3 ways” is also a power phrase.
Anything with a number is powerful.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 12 --

3
Anything with a number is powerful. So, think of things with numbers,
like “7 tips to blank.” The other power words are “quickly and easily.”
That’s also, by the way, the outrageous result, is lose weight.
So, I’ve got “3 ways to lose weight quickly and easily.” If you wanted to
get really specific, you could reverse that and say, “3 ways to quickly
and easily lose weight,” to get the power words and the outrageous
result. The outrageous result here is just losing weight.
Now, if I wanted to get even more carried away, I could say, “3 ways to
drop all of your excess body fat. Get really exaggerated. It’s
outrageous results, okay? Quickly and easily. And then I put the
minus. What does it minus? I start with the word “without.” In
previous videos, we’ve already covered the power of using the word
“without.”
“Without” – in other words, what I’m subtracting – “giving up your
favorite foods or spending hours doing boring cardio,” I know that, for a
fact, is my public enemies #1 and 2.
So, that’s what you want to do for your headline. Think of what the #1
enemies for your reader and your prospect are. Write them down.
Think of a power word or power phrase – in this case, “3 ways is a
power phrase, quickly and easily,” all those are power words.”
“Revealed, new, we’ve gone over power words before. “New,
scientifically-proven way to lose weight, quickly and easily.” That’s
tons of power words right there in the middle, including an outrageous
benefit, because the outrageous result is “the scientific way to lose
weight quickly and easily.” And then, the public enemies.
You will use this formula over and over again, throughout writing any
marketing materials that you use. And, it works. It works like a charm.
Then there’s the “pain, then pleasure principle.” What do I mean by
that?
You want to always reinforce your prospects pain, and follow it with
your specific solution. Not a solution, your specific solution.
Here’s an example. I’m taking a service as an example. “The greatest
thing about our dry cleaning service is that we’re reliable, which means
you’ll never have to come back and say, “My shirt is ruined.”
If anyone takes their clothes to the dry cleaners, which is most people
listening to this video, you’re going to experience “My shirt has been
ruined. You just ruined my suit. This dry cleaner just wrecked a $500
shirt,” or whatever the case may be.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 12 --

4
So, if you’re selling the fact that your dry cleaning service is reliable,
that’s your specific solution to the problem. “The best thing about our
dry cleaning service” – you’re not saying about any dry cleaning
service – “our dry cleaning service, is that we’re reliable.” You can
even go into more details there. We actually won the most reliable dry
cleaning services in the world award,” or whatever. “That means that
you will never have to come back and say, “My shirt is ruined.”
What I did is I turned the formula a little bit around, just to show you
how flexible this is. I started with the positive thing, our dry cleaning
service is reliable, but I reinforced the pain.
You want to be thinking this way throughout the entire video sales
letter. Am I reinforcing the pain? It’s just something that you have to
continually remind your reader about. They know they’re in pain, but
they need to be reminded about it.
So, what are some of the painful aspects, in this case, of dry cleaning?
Well, ruined shirts, ruined clothes, etc. Reliability solves that problem.
That is the “pain, then pleasure principle.” You’re just going to use it
throughout the video. Not every sentence, of course, but you want to
be thinking of ways that you can always reinforce the pain.
Then, there’s what I call the triple guarantee. The triple guarantee is a
really cool way of creating a guarantee. Almost everybody watching
me right now knows they need to have a guarantee for their video
sales letter, or for their sales page, or for anything, for that matter.
I developed this little formula called the triple guarantee. When
wording your guarantee, what you want to do is come up with 3
specific ways you give your customer that they can get their money
back. Instead of just saying, “We have an iron-clad guarantee. After
30 days, if you’re not satisfied, just call us and we’ll give you your
money back.” That’s your typical guarantee.
We’re going to put that on steroids, and we’re going to make it a triple
guarantee. Here’s an example. “Here’s my 100% iron-clad, triple
guarantee.” By the way, you want to say “triple guarantee,” because it
stands out like a sore thumb.
“If you don’t see blank, whatever that may be, product or service
benefits, if you don’t feel or experience blank, more product benefits,”
and this is the best part, “or even if you don’t like the font I used in the
book, then I’ll give you your money back after 30 days.” Then, your
guarantee specifics come in here.
Look what I’ve done here. I’ve created 3 things: see, feel. Again,
what am I using? I’m using sensory words. I’m trying to appeal to the
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 12 --

5
senses that we talked about in the earlier videos. Experiential.
Everything’s an experiential thing.
So, I want my user to know that, “If you don’t see the results that were
promised, if you don’t see the weight falling off your body, if you don’t
feel and experience more energy and more life-giving energy and
vitality than you’ve ever felt, or even if you don’t like the font I used in
the book.”
The third one is something ridiculous. You can say, “Even if you don’t
like my haircut.” Say something funny. It also breaks the pattern. A
little bit of a pattern interrupt within the guarantee, when you do that.
The guarantee is going to be right toward the end of the pitch, where
you’re saying, “Here’s how much it’s going to cost you. Don’t worry,
it’s guaranteed.”
We’re going to go over the flow of that in the next few videos.
When you do that, they’re in this, “Should I buy this?” They get back
into that whole decision mode. This helps pull people out of that
decision mode. It makes them chuckle a little bit, especially if you use
something really absurd.
That is the triple guarantee. Use it whenever possible.
The faux price. There are 2 variations on the faux price, 2 steps to
doing it.
Step one: You want to anchor a higher price to your product before
revealing your bonuses and the actual price. It’s a very, very proven
technique. So, if your product sells for say $47, what I’m asking you to
do is to do this. “Blank,” the name of your product, “is sold for,” the
faux price. I often use the term “is valued at” rather than “sold for.” But
in case yours actually is sold for this and it actually is going on a
discount for this, you can use “sold for.”
If not, “the name of product has a value of,” and then I’ll put a value of
about 2 to 3 times higher. I’ll say $97 for a $47 ebook.
This may sound like, “Whoa, I’m going to lose people here.” Hang with
me, you’re not. You say, “Every Other Day Diet has a value of $97,
and for good reason; because it includes,” and then I include all of the
stuff that it includes. It includes step-by-step instruction on what to do,
blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, everything it includes.
“And before I reveal your better deal today, let me cover all of the
bonuses that you’re about to receive. That’s step one.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 12 --

6
So, you’ve anchored a faux price. It’s $97. You just said that they
were going to get a better deal today, so they don’t know if that means
a better price or that you’re just going to give them all of these
bonuses. They’re thinking, in their mind, $97 when it’s $47. Or they’re
thinking in their mind that it’s $3,000 when it’s only $1,000. Whatever it
may be, 2 to 3 times higher. That’s called a faux price.
And then, you go into the “Oh, I’m going to tell you more about your
better deal later. Let me cover the bonuses.” We’ll get into structure
and bonuses in another video.
Step 2 of this faux price performance thing we’re doing here is to
perform a price drop right before the actual price, and one that includes
the faux price plus bonus value.
Here’s what I mean by that.
Like I said earlier, “name of your product normally costs or has a value
of $97.” That’s my faux price, but your faux price may be different. “If I
were to sell you all of the bonuses I’ve covered separately, the value
would actually be,” and then I multiply the number times 10 or 5. I pick
a realistic number, what I would really sell all of those bonuses for.
“It’s $397. This is worth $397, yet you’re not going to pay that. You’re
not going to pay $397, or $297, or $10,000, whatever your $X may be.
You’re not even going to be $Y.” You’re going to drop that price down
in half. “And you’re not even going to pay $Z.” You’re going to drop it
again.
“And this is the best part. In fact, by ordering today, exclusively from
this video, you’re not even going to pay our normal price of $97. And
once again, you can say, our regular value price of $97. You’ll not
even pay what the book is actually worth, $97.”
Then you go into what it actually is. “You’re going to only pay blank.”
And at that point, your sales are going to increase because people are
really anchored to that $97 original price, and once you drop it and
build it up with the benefits and the bonuses that you’re going to get
with it, and you start dropping that price value, and then it goes below
that faux price, the deal triggers go off. “Wow, this is an awesome
deal.” That’s called a faux price.
The next VSL toolbox tool that we have for you is called product
pacing. It’s kind of like future pacing, except with a product.
All we ask here is that you always assume that the prospect has or is
about to purchase the product. Has or is about to purchase the
product.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 12 --

7
An example of this: “When you purchase whatever the product is
today, you will want to turn immediately to page 37, because you just
have to see blank, whatever is on page 37.” Obviously, you want to
pick a page out of your book.
“This is a service. When you order the blank service today, you’re
going to want to immediately call blank,” or you’re going to want to tell
them what they want to do first, if it’s a service.
If it’s a book, you tell them to turn to a page. If it’s a video, you tell
them to watch at a certain frame point, and you pull out an important
segment. “You just have to see this technique for losing weight,”
whatever it may be.
But notice the phraseology. “When you purchase today.” I’m not
saying, “If you purchase today.” “You will want to turn immediately to
page 37. I am future pacing. When you purchase, turn to page 37.”
Same thing works for services, same things work for any product you
can think of. Just try to think of future pacing. We call that product
pacing, because it deals specifically with the product.
Next up is pre-qualification. What this does is kick the tire-kickers out
of the club. This is incredibly important to do, because you don’t want
tire-kickers in your club. You don’t want people just watching the video
and going, “Ah, okay, whatever.” The way to do this is to phrase it
something like this. “Before I tell you about the success others are
seeing with,” whatever your product or service is, “let me first tell you
who would never fit as a product/service here customer. Let me tell
you who would never, ever work as a subscriber to fill-in-the-blank
service, as a reader of fill-in-the-blank book, who will never fit.” And
you go into the people that will never fit your product model.
For my case, it would be this. “This will never work for people that are
looking to swallow a pill and miraculously lose 40 pounds overnight.
This will never, ever work for someone who thinks that all they can do
is take some magic green juice and presto, chango, they can eat
whatever they want and lose weight. It’s never going to work if you
have absolutely zero self-discipline. I know that you have self-
discipline, because you’re watching this video.”
So, I’ll walk through the reasons why it will not work.” What I’m doing
is I’m pre-qualifying. The reason I’m doing this is because no one
wants to feel left out. No one wants to feel left out of a club.
So, if you say, “Before I tell you about the success others are seeing,
others are experiencing with” whatever your product or service is,”
you’ve already set yourself up as saying, “This is the club. The club is
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 12 --

8
a success club, up here. We’re up here. Before I tell you about all of
the great success these people are having, I want to tell you who will
never work.” People will say, “Oh, that’s cool. I fit that bill. I can do
that.”
This is what you want your reader to be thinking. “I’m not looking for a
pill I can swallow and lose 48 pounds overnight. That’d be great, but I
know that’s not realistic.” I’ll even reinforce those statements and say,
“Look, you know. You’re a smart person. You know that’s not realistic.
You’re tired of the lies, right?”
So, I’ll say something to make it a caveat to that statement, and come
back to it that way.
I’m building up a whole list of reasons why the people that would not
want as clients, to be honest with you, but also the people that I know
aren’t going to buy. This is the people that would never fit.
The great thing about that is it makes the people who are on the fence
want to join the club. They want to be a part of the success club.
The next tool is wordsmithing. This is something we’ve talked about in
previous videos. Wordsmithing is basically just clever copywriting
without needing to be a copywriter, per say. This is just taking a
sentence and putting some spice on it.
What I’ve done here is given you 3 examples. On the next slide, I want
to show you. But basically, the examples are built around getting rid of
trite and boring words, and replacing them with power words and
provocative phrases.
Okay? It’s as simple as that.
Let me give you an example of really bad wordsmithing: “Product or
service is really good, if you want to make money on the internet.”
Obviously, this is a make money biz op product, and the name of the
product is really good if you want to make money on the internet.”
That’s really bad wordsmithing. But it’s okay to start there.
A wordsmith comes in and tweaks a few things. This is better.
“Product is ideal if you really want to make money on the internet.”
Now, what did I change? I went from “really good” to “ideal.” Right?
Not too difficult to figure out. Not too difficult to do. “Really good” to
“ideal.” You want to be thinking of phrases like that, like “ideal” is killer.
Just take out the “really good,” which is a really bland, trite way of
saying things. “If you really want to make money on the internet.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 12 --

9
I’ve also added the word “really.” “If you want to make money on the
internet, if you want to lose weight, if you want to have your quilting
service taken care of.” No. “If you really want.”
So, wordsmithing involves clever use of words, but it’s really not
difficult. Changing “good” to “ideal.”
This is going to happen when you go back over your video sales letter
script a few times. You’re going to go, “You know what? I can change
‘good’ to ‘awesome.’ I think that will work. Or I can change ‘you want’
to ‘you really want,’ because that’s implying something.” That’s
wordsmithing.
Let me give you the ideal, supreme wordsmithing. Notice I didn’t go
with best. I substituted “best” for “supreme.” I’m a wordsmither, man.
So, “Product is ideally positioned to empower you to make a truckload
of cash on the internet, and do it faster than ever.”
Now, compare that to the first sentence and tell me which one you
would rather buy.
All I’ve done is taken that first sentence and wordsmithed it. I’ve just
added some cool little words, “positioned,” “empowered,” and catch
phrases, “truckload of cash,” “boatload of cash,” “tons of money,”
whatever you want to call it. “Tons of money” is kind of trite, so I think
“a truckload of cash” as less trite. If I thought some more, I could
probably think of some even less trite phrases than that.
But the idea is very powerful here. Product is ideally positioned. It’s
not just “ideal,” it’s “ideally positioned,” which makes it sound even that
much more exclusive, that much more tailored to their needs.
“To empower.” “Empower” is an awesome power word. Please write
that one down. “Empower you,” rather than “to help you.” If you say,
“to help you,” “to let you.” No, “to empower you.” Empowerment is a
very good word.
“To make a truckload of cash, and do it faster.” I added the “do it
faster than ever,” because that makes it even much more strong.
That is wordsmithing. You’re going to be doing that throughout your
video sales letter, and I think you’ll find it a pretty simple exercise.
The next step is subliminal highlighting. What this is, is we’re going to
specifically highlight specific words in red throughout your VSL, just
like I’ve done in this one sentence right here. The word “red” is
obviously highlighted.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 12 --

10
The question is, which word? You want to make specific words
highlighted, to prompt your readers subconscious mind.
See, consciously, few readers are going to notice this. They’re going
to notice that there’s a word that’s red there, but they’re not going to
form a pattern with it. The words that you’re going to be using, you’re
going to pull out specific words that appeal to your prospects greatest
desires. That’s not always the product, that’s not always the service.
That’s also universal desires.
Let me give you some examples.
Easy. Fun. Simple. Superior. Proven. Only. Here’s one I really like
to use: “yes” and “buy” and “now.” In fact, I will use 3 slides. This is
an advanced ninja tactic. I’ll use 3 slides, and across those 3 slides, I
will highlight “yes” on the first slide, “buy” on the next slide, and “now”
on the third slide.
Your conscious mind will never pick this up. Your subconscious just
read “yes, buy now.” That’s pretty ninja.
You don’t have to think in terms like this. I’ve actually built like 15- and
20-word sentences that go across 20 slides before. It’s pretty cool to
do. It’s a lot of fun to have after you get your video sales letter done.
But for now, just worry about highlighting those power words, those
really specific words that pull out the greatest desires.
Everyone wants something that’s done for them, that’s easy, that’s
simple, that’s fun, that’s proven, that has a lot of evidence behind it.
Those kind of things are the words that you want to highlight.
Next up is title-case formatting, and that is exactly what you see in the
headline right underneath “3X VSL toolbox.” That is a title-case
formatting, where the first letter of the word is capital.
The reason this is cool is you’re writing every slide like a headline. If
you look at headlines in most pages that are text-based or otherwise,
most headlines have the first letter capitalized of every word. If you’ve
ever wondered why, it’s because guys like Marketing Sherpa and other
people have proven that those convert better.
Why? Don’t know. Don’t know why they convert better, but they do.
It’s still something you want to test, because on some audiences it
doesn’t convert better. So, you want to test it.
But basically, when you do this, you’re turning your video sales letter
into 100 slides of headlines. The irony here is that people read
headlines before they read anything else.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 12 --

11
So, on a subconscious level, they’re thinking, “Headline, headline,
headline, headline,” over 100 different slides or more. It’s a very
powerful trick. Very powerful trick.
Like I said, you have to test it. You would test it against normal case,
which is how that sentence is written right there, in normal case. It’s
something that certain audiences just kill it, and in certain audiences it
just doesn’t work as well. That’s one of the very first split tests we
suggest that people do.
Next up is the one sentence formatting trick. This is so easy. Limit
90% of your slides to only one sentence followed by an ellipsis. That’s
it. That’s it; 90% of your slides are going to be only one sentence.
If you write a paragraph with 8 sentences, how many slides are you
going to have? 8.
When people ask, “How many slides should I build?” How many
sentences do you have? It’s pretty simple. That’s 90% of your VSL.
I’ll cover what the other 10% is in another session, but 90% of it is
going to be one sentence, and there’s a reason for this. When slides
go by quickly, it creates a hypnotic effect.
Your slides are going to go by very quickly, with one sentence at a
time. People are going to want to, “Whoa, I’m reading. I’d better
concentrate. This is going pretty quick.” This is what you want.
They’re listening to what you’re saying, but they’re also being slightly
hypnotized in the process by what they’re seeing. It’s pretty cool.
Rather than having one long slide with tons of text on it, I’ll break the
video sales letter up and have some slides like that, but most of them,
90%, have one sentence only.
We’re going to conclude this session today with looking over a few
things to make sure that you’ve done to wrap up your USP. We’re
going to put a bow on it. We’re going to color this done.
Here’s what we’re going to do. I’m going to ask you a few questions.
Did you run your USP by your friends, by your colleagues, and even by
your buyers list? If so, how did their feedback alter your USP, if at all?
Did your buyer say, “Oh, I think the value of your company, the primary
benefits are blank, blank and blank, the problems that is solves are
blank, blank, and blank,” and your blanks were completely different?”
If so, hope you adjusted your USP.
Who do you want to cater to: yourself or your buyers? Your buyers. It
doesn’t matter what you think. It matters what your buyers think.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 12 --

12
Fortunately, most of the time, you’re pretty dead-on with most of the
things your buyers want. But in some cases, it’s night and day. That’s
why we suggest you always poll a buyers list.
If you don’t have one, ask your friends or colleagues, people that you
respect in business, just like we covered in that very first session, the
homework assignment.
Have you trimmed your USP down to 20 to 25 words or less? That is
an exercise in diligence. It’s easy to write a USP that’s 50 words long.
It’s much more difficult to trim that down to 20 to 25. I like 20; 25 is the
absolute maximum. Get it down to 25 words. You want to be able to
just rattle it off in an elevator.
Next up, you want to remember one thing. Your USP must be clear,
must contain your top benefits, and highlight your top benefit as your
primary benefit.
So, the focus of your USP, again, is your primary benefit. As long as
that is focused and you follow the USP factory formula, it should be
wrapped up and done. Just make sure you get that feedback from
people that you trust, your friends, preferably your colleagues in
business, and the ultimate way would be to get it from your buyers list.
Once you do that, you can wrap up the USP, you can call it quits.
You can come back and alter it later, but you’ve got one that’s
functional and that will work to produce your entire video sales letter.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 12 --
